      • 7.4 Transformer

      • 8.2 Region Classification
      • 8.3 Event Detection

    • Complexity / Payoff Summary

ML Roadmap

A progressive plan for EDA, preprocessing, and model training on the RLCS 2024 dataset. The guiding principle: get to a working end-to-end pipeline as quickly as possible, then iterate toward more complex tasks and models.

Current State

  • ~7,300 RLCS 2024 replays parsed and stored on S3 as Parquet files
  • Each replay: (frames, features) float32 array at a fixed FPS, stored as a Parquet file
  • ReplayDataset / WindowedReplayDataset provide the data access layer
  • Local impulse.db may be stale — sync before starting

Phase 0: Environment Prep

These are prerequisites before any EDA or training work.

Phase 1: Dataset-Level EDA

Goal: Understand the shape and health of the full dataset without loading any Parquet files. All of this is available directly from impulse.db.

1.1 Replay-Level Statistics (DB only)

Query parsed_replays and raw_replays to produce:

Metric How
Total replays, total frames dataset.get_frame_count_summary()
Frame count distribution histogram of frame_count
Game duration distribution frame_count / fps
File size distribution histogram of file_size_bytes
FPS distribution should be uniform; verify
Feature count distribution should be uniform; flag outliers
Parse failure rate db.get_parse_stats()

Red flags to look for: - Replays with very few frames (< 300) — likely corrupted or forfeit matches - Bimodal duration distribution — may indicate regulation vs. overtime games - Inconsistent feature counts — schema changed mid-parse - High failure rate — worth investigating before training

1.2 Metadata-Level Statistics

The metadata JSON column (from Ballchasing) contains rich per-replay information. Parse it across the full dataset to produce:

Metric Notes
Overtime rate % of games with overtime_seconds > 0
Goal count distribution total goals per game
Team size distribution should be all 3v3 for RLCS
Season / event distribution from group hierarchy
Region distribution NA, EU, SAM, MENA, OCE, SSA, APAC
Arena distribution useful to know; may affect physics
Date distribution verify temporal spread

This metadata will also provide labels for future supervised tasks (region, outcome, etc.).

Phase 2: Single-Replay Schema Inspection

Goal: Understand exactly what the feature columns are before touching anything at scale.

Load 1–2 replays and inspect:

replay = dataset.load_replay(some_id)
print(replay.frames.columns.tolist())   # feature names
print(replay.frames.dtypes)             # all should be float32
print(replay.frames.describe())         # value ranges, NaN counts

Things to document: - Full list of column names (ball features, per-player features, global features) - Which columns represent position (x, y, z), velocity, rotation, boost - Whether player columns are prefixed by player index or player name - Whether NaNs appear and where (e.g., boost before first pickup, dead frames) - Units and approximate value ranges for each feature group

Plot time series for a few features over one replay: - Ball position x/y/z - Player 0 boost amount - Player 0 speed (magnitude of velocity)

This step produces the feature reference you’ll use when designing preprocessing.

Phase 3: Feature-Level EDA

Goal: Understand the statistical structure of the data before modeling.

Work on a sample of ~100–200 replays (use dataset.load_sample(200, seed=42)).

3.1 Feature Statistics Across Replays

For each feature column, compute across all sampled frames: - Mean, std, min, max, median - NaN rate - Autocorrelation at lag 1, 5, 10 (measures temporal smoothness)

3.2 Correlation Analysis

  • Feature-feature correlation matrix (on a random frame sample)
  • Identify highly correlated features (candidates for removal)
  • Check ball position vs. player position correlation

3.3 Distribution Inspection

  • Histograms for key features (boost, speed, position components)
  • Check for heavy tails, hard bounds (boost is clamped to [0, 100]), bimodality
  • Compare distributions across regions (if labels are extracted)

3.4 Temporal Patterns

  • Average ball height over game time (does ball stay low or go up?)
  • Average player speed over game time (fatigue / pacing)
  • Goal rate over game time (are goals more likely near the end?)

Phase 4: Preprocessing

Goal: Produce a clean, normalized feature matrix ready for modeling.

4.1 Feature Selection

Decide which features to keep for the first model:

Feature group Keep? Notes
Ball position (x, y, z) Yes Core state
Ball velocity (vx, vy, vz) Yes Core state
Ball rotation Maybe Less informative for most tasks
Player position Yes Core state
Player velocity Yes Core state
Player rotation Maybe Quaternion vs Euler — pick one
Player boost Yes Important game mechanic
CurrentTime Maybe Useful for temporal context; adds complexity

Start minimal: ball position + velocity, player positions + velocities + boost. Add features back if model performance is unsatisfying.

4.2 Handling NaNs

  • Identify which features have NaNs and why (e.g., boost before first pickup)
  • Strategy options:
    • Forward-fill: carry last valid value forward (reasonable for most physics features)
    • Zero-fill: treat missing as 0 (may be wrong for positions)
    • Drop frames with NaNs (simplest; may lose data near game start)
  • Pick one strategy and apply consistently

4.3 Normalization

Use physical bounds scaling as the primary normalization approach — divide each feature by its known physical maximum so that values land in approximately [-1, 1] or [0, 1]:

Feature group Divide by Result range
Position x 4096 [-1, 1]
Position y 5120 [-1, 1]
Position z 2044 [-1, 1]
Ball linear velocity 6000 [-1, 1]
Player linear velocity 2300 [-1, 1]
Ball angular velocity 5.5 [-1, 1]
Player angular velocity 5.5 [-1, 1]
Boost 255 [0, 1]
Quaternion components (none) already [-1, 1]

Rationale for preferring physical bounds over z-score normalization: - The zero point is semantically meaningful (e.g., center of field, zero velocity), so centering by subtracting the empirical mean would destroy that. - No fitting is required, eliminating any data leakage risk from computing statistics on the training set. - The distributions are non-normal (e.g., ball y-velocity is bimodal since play concentrates near each team’s goal), so z-score does not produce standard-normal outputs anyway.

Important: normalization must be applied consistently to all models including baselines. Normalize before building any X/Y arrays, not inside the model or the training loop.

4.4 Train / Val / Test Split

Split at the replay level, not the frame level, to avoid data leakage:

# Stratify by region if labels are available
train_ids, test_ids = train_test_split(all_ids, test_size=0.1, random_state=42)
train_ids, val_ids  = train_test_split(train_ids, test_size=0.1, random_state=42)

Do not shuffle frames within a replay — temporal order matters.

Phase 5: First Model — Next-Frame Linear Regression

Goal: Get a working end-to-end training loop with the simplest possible model.

Task Definition

Given frame \(t\) (a vector of \(F\) features), predict frame \(t+1\).

This is self-supervised — no labels needed. The target is always the next row in the same Parquet file. It is the simplest possible temporal task and serves as both a sanity check and a baseline for more complex models.

5.1 Sklearn Baseline

Train on a flat sample of windows (no DataLoader needed at this stage):

# Build X (inputs) and Y (targets) from a sample of replays
X, Y = [], []
for replay in dataset.load_sample(200, seed=42):
    arr = replay.frames.to_numpy(dtype=np.float32)
    X.append(arr[:-1])   # all frames except last
    Y.append(arr[1:])    # all frames except first

X = np.concatenate(X)   # (total_frames, features)
Y = np.concatenate(Y)

from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_norm = scaler.fit_transform(X)

model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
model.fit(X_norm, Y)

5.2 Evaluation

Per-feature mean squared error:

Y_pred = model.predict(X_val_norm)
mse_per_feature = ((Y_val - Y_pred) ** 2).mean(axis=0)

Interpret results: - Which features are easy to predict (low MSE)? Likely: boost (slow-changing), position - Which are hard? Likely: velocity (discontinuous at collisions), rotation - Is the linear model actually doing better than a trivial “predict no change” baseline?

Trivial baseline: \(\hat{y}_{t+1} = y_t\) (predict no change). Compute its MSE too.

5.3 What Good Looks Like

At this stage, success means: 1. The pipeline runs end-to-end without errors 2. The linear model beats the trivial baseline on at least some features 3. You can inspect per-feature errors and understand why some are harder

Phase 6: Scaling to Full Dataset with PyTorch

Goal: Train on all 7,300 replays using WindowedReplayDataset + DataLoader.

6.1 Setup

from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from impulse.replay_dataset import WindowedReplayDataset, ReplayDataset

dataset = ReplayDataset(db_path='./impulse.db', s3_manager=s3, cache_dir='/data/parsed')
windowed = WindowedReplayDataset(dataset, window_size=1, stride=4, cache_size=32)

loader = DataLoader(windowed, batch_size=2048, shuffle=True,
                    num_workers=4, pin_memory=True)

Note: window_size=1, stride=4 gives one-frame samples (for next-frame regression, you need to handle the target shift inside the training loop or use window_size=2 and split into input/target inside the model).

6.2 Training Loop (PyTorch Linear Model)

import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LinearPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = nn.Linear(n_features, n_features)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.linear(x)

Train with MSE loss, Adam optimizer, and a learning rate scheduler.

6.3 Storage Considerations

For training on EC2, all 7,300 files need to be local or very fast to access: - EBS gp3 volume (~50 GB, ~$4/month): mount once, download all Parquet files, train repeatedly. Best option for iterative training. - Instance store NVMe: fastest, but ephemeral — re-download on every restart. - Direct S3 streaming: fine for EDA, slow for training (network bottleneck).

Recommendation: use an EBS volume with cache_dir pointing to it.

Phase 7: Model Complexity Ladder

Once the linear baseline is working, progress along this ladder:

7.1 MLP

Replace the linear layer with a small feedforward network:

class MLPPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features, hidden=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(n_features, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, n_features),
        )

The MLP can learn nonlinear interactions but still treats each frame independently.

7.2 Multi-Step Input Window

Instead of predicting from a single frame, use a window of \(k\) past frames:

  • Change WindowedReplayDataset(window_size=k+1) and split in the training loop: x = window[:k], y = window[k]
  • The input to the model becomes (batch, k, features) — a sequence
  • An MLP can still handle this by flattening: (batch, k * features)

7.3 RNN / LSTM

Natural next step after multi-step input:

class LSTMPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features, hidden=256, n_layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(n_features, hidden, n_layers, batch_first=True)
        self.head = nn.Linear(hidden, n_features)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len, features)
        out, _ = self.lstm(x)
        return self.head(out[:, -1, :])  # predict from last hidden state

LSTMs can model long-range temporal dependencies (e.g., player intent, play patterns). Expect training to be significantly slower — use GPU.

7.4 Transformer

After LSTM, try a causal Transformer encoder: - Self-attention over the input window - Can be faster than LSTM on GPU due to parallelism - Requires more hyperparameter tuning (heads, layers, dropout)

Phase 8: Supervised Tasks

Once the forecasting pipeline is solid, tackle supervised tasks that require labels.

8.1 Outcome Prediction

Task: predict which team wins (binary classification) from the game state at a given time point or aggregate statistics.

Labels: extract from Ballchasing metadata (blue/orange score at end of game). A replay is a blue win if blue score > orange score at game end.

Features: aggregate statistics (mean/std/max per team per feature) — no temporal model needed for a baseline.

Metrics: accuracy, AUC-ROC. Baseline: always predict the team with more boost.

8.2 Region Classification

Task: classify a replay by the region it was played in (NA, EU, SAM, MENA, OCE, SSA, APAC).

Labels: extract from the group hierarchy path in the S3 key or Ballchasing metadata.

Approach: - Start with aggregate statistics + logistic regression (no sequence model needed) - Then try sequence-level classification with LSTM or Transformer encoder + classification head

Caveat: this task is easier than it sounds (arena selection differs by region, team playstyles differ) but harder than it looks (7 unbalanced classes, some regions have fewer games). Verify class balance before training.

8.3 Event Detection

Task: predict binary events — “a goal will occur in the next \(k\) seconds” — as a per-frame classification problem.

This is a harder task but naturally fits the sliding window framework and could be useful for downstream analysis tools.

Phase 9: Infrastructure Improvements for Scale

As the dataset and models grow:

  • Pre-converted format: convert all Parquet files to memory-mapped numpy arrays (np.memmap) or WebDataset .tar shards for faster I/O at very large scale (100k+ replays)
  • Feature engineering pipeline: add a preprocessing step that saves normalized arrays so normalization doesn’t happen at training time
  • Distributed training: if a single GPU is insufficient, move to multi-GPU with DistributedDataParallel or use a managed training service
  • Experiment tracking: add MLflow or Weights & Biases for tracking runs, metrics, and model artifacts
  • Model versioning: save model checkpoints and normalization stats together so inference uses the same preprocessing as training

Complexity / Payoff Summary

Phase Complexity Payoff
DB-level EDA Very low Understand dataset health and structure
Schema inspection Very low Know your features
Feature EDA Low Inform preprocessing and modeling
Preprocessing Medium Correctness matters; do this carefully
Linear regression baseline Low End-to-end pipeline working
PyTorch training loop Medium Full-scale training on 7k replays
MLP Low delta Immediate nonlinear improvement
Multi-step input Low delta Temporal context
LSTM Medium Long-range temporal modeling
Transformer High State-of-the-art sequence modeling
Outcome prediction Medium Meaningful supervised task
Region classification Medium Fun; interpretable
Event detection High Useful downstream; complex labels
Source Code
# ML Roadmap

A progressive plan for EDA, preprocessing, and model training on the RLCS 2024 dataset.
The guiding principle: get to a working end-to-end pipeline as quickly as possible,
then iterate toward more complex tasks and models.

---

## Current State

- ~7,300 RLCS 2024 replays parsed and stored on S3 as Parquet files
- Each replay: `(frames, features)` float32 array at a fixed FPS, stored as a Parquet file
- `ReplayDataset` / `WindowedReplayDataset` provide the data access layer
- Local `impulse.db` may be stale — sync before starting

---

## Phase 0: Environment Prep

These are prerequisites before any EDA or training work.

- [ ] **Sync the database**: `db.pull()` to get the latest parse state from S3
- [ ] **Verify `s3fs` is installed**: required for direct S3 streaming via `pd.read_parquet('s3://...')`
      — or always use `cache_dir` and skip `s3fs` entirely
- [ ] **Choose EDA environment**: local Jupyter (streaming from S3) vs. EC2 (files already nearby)
- [ ] **Verify dataset size**: `len(dataset)`, frame count distribution, total data volume

---

## Phase 1: Dataset-Level EDA

**Goal**: Understand the shape and health of the full dataset without loading any Parquet files.
All of this is available directly from `impulse.db`.

### 1.1 Replay-Level Statistics (DB only)

Query `parsed_replays` and `raw_replays` to produce:

| Metric | How |
|---|---|
| Total replays, total frames | `dataset.get_frame_count_summary()` |
| Frame count distribution | histogram of `frame_count` |
| Game duration distribution | `frame_count / fps` |
| File size distribution | histogram of `file_size_bytes` |
| FPS distribution | should be uniform; verify |
| Feature count distribution | should be uniform; flag outliers |
| Parse failure rate | `db.get_parse_stats()` |

Red flags to look for:
- Replays with very few frames (< 300) — likely corrupted or forfeit matches
- Bimodal duration distribution — may indicate regulation vs. overtime games
- Inconsistent feature counts — schema changed mid-parse
- High failure rate — worth investigating before training

### 1.2 Metadata-Level Statistics

The `metadata` JSON column (from Ballchasing) contains rich per-replay information.
Parse it across the full dataset to produce:

| Metric | Notes |
|---|---|
| Overtime rate | % of games with `overtime_seconds > 0` |
| Goal count distribution | total goals per game |
| Team size distribution | should be all 3v3 for RLCS |
| Season / event distribution | from group hierarchy |
| Region distribution | NA, EU, SAM, MENA, OCE, SSA, APAC |
| Arena distribution | useful to know; may affect physics |
| Date distribution | verify temporal spread |

This metadata will also provide labels for future supervised tasks (region, outcome, etc.).

---

## Phase 2: Single-Replay Schema Inspection

**Goal**: Understand exactly what the feature columns are before touching anything at scale.

Load 1–2 replays and inspect:

```python
replay = dataset.load_replay(some_id)
print(replay.frames.columns.tolist())   # feature names
print(replay.frames.dtypes)             # all should be float32
print(replay.frames.describe())         # value ranges, NaN counts
```

Things to document:
- Full list of column names (ball features, per-player features, global features)
- Which columns represent position (x, y, z), velocity, rotation, boost
- Whether player columns are prefixed by player index or player name
- Whether NaNs appear and where (e.g., boost before first pickup, dead frames)
- Units and approximate value ranges for each feature group

Plot time series for a few features over one replay:
- Ball position x/y/z
- Player 0 boost amount
- Player 0 speed (magnitude of velocity)

This step produces the **feature reference** you'll use when designing preprocessing.

---

## Phase 3: Feature-Level EDA

**Goal**: Understand the statistical structure of the data before modeling.

Work on a sample of ~100–200 replays (use `dataset.load_sample(200, seed=42)`).

### 3.1 Feature Statistics Across Replays

For each feature column, compute across all sampled frames:
- Mean, std, min, max, median
- NaN rate
- Autocorrelation at lag 1, 5, 10 (measures temporal smoothness)

### 3.2 Correlation Analysis

- Feature-feature correlation matrix (on a random frame sample)
- Identify highly correlated features (candidates for removal)
- Check ball position vs. player position correlation

### 3.3 Distribution Inspection

- Histograms for key features (boost, speed, position components)
- Check for heavy tails, hard bounds (boost is clamped to [0, 100]), bimodality
- Compare distributions across regions (if labels are extracted)

### 3.4 Temporal Patterns

- Average ball height over game time (does ball stay low or go up?)
- Average player speed over game time (fatigue / pacing)
- Goal rate over game time (are goals more likely near the end?)

---

## Phase 4: Preprocessing

**Goal**: Produce a clean, normalized feature matrix ready for modeling.

### 4.1 Feature Selection

Decide which features to keep for the first model:

| Feature group | Keep? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Ball position (x, y, z) | Yes | Core state |
| Ball velocity (vx, vy, vz) | Yes | Core state |
| Ball rotation | Maybe | Less informative for most tasks |
| Player position | Yes | Core state |
| Player velocity | Yes | Core state |
| Player rotation | Maybe | Quaternion vs Euler — pick one |
| Player boost | Yes | Important game mechanic |
| CurrentTime | Maybe | Useful for temporal context; adds complexity |

Start minimal: ball position + velocity, player positions + velocities + boost.
Add features back if model performance is unsatisfying.

### 4.2 Handling NaNs

- Identify which features have NaNs and why (e.g., boost before first pickup)
- Strategy options:
  - Forward-fill: carry last valid value forward (reasonable for most physics features)
  - Zero-fill: treat missing as 0 (may be wrong for positions)
  - Drop frames with NaNs (simplest; may lose data near game start)
- Pick one strategy and apply consistently

### 4.3 Normalization

Use **physical bounds scaling** as the primary normalization approach — divide
each feature by its known physical maximum so that values land in approximately
[-1, 1] or [0, 1]:

| Feature group          | Divide by | Result range    |
|------------------------|-----------|-----------------|
| Position x             | 4096      | [-1, 1]         |
| Position y             | 5120      | [-1, 1]         |
| Position z             | 2044      | [-1, 1]         |
| Ball linear velocity   | 6000      | [-1, 1]         |
| Player linear velocity | 2300      | [-1, 1]         |
| Ball angular velocity  | 5.5       | [-1, 1]         |
| Player angular velocity| 5.5       | [-1, 1]         |
| Boost                  | 255       | [0, 1]          |
| Quaternion components  | (none)    | already [-1, 1] |

Rationale for preferring physical bounds over z-score normalization:
- The zero point is semantically meaningful (e.g., center of field, zero
  velocity), so centering by subtracting the empirical mean would destroy that.
- No fitting is required, eliminating any data leakage risk from computing
  statistics on the training set.
- The distributions are non-normal (e.g., ball y-velocity is bimodal since play
  concentrates near each team's goal), so z-score does not produce
  standard-normal outputs anyway.

**Important**: normalization must be applied consistently to **all** models
including baselines. Normalize before building any X/Y arrays, not inside the
model or the training loop.

### 4.4 Train / Val / Test Split

Split at the **replay level**, not the frame level, to avoid data leakage:

```python
# Stratify by region if labels are available
train_ids, test_ids = train_test_split(all_ids, test_size=0.1, random_state=42)
train_ids, val_ids  = train_test_split(train_ids, test_size=0.1, random_state=42)
```

Do not shuffle frames within a replay — temporal order matters.

---

## Phase 5: First Model — Next-Frame Linear Regression

**Goal**: Get a working end-to-end training loop with the simplest possible model.

### Task Definition

Given frame $t$ (a vector of $F$ features), predict frame $t+1$.

This is self-supervised — no labels needed. The target is always the next row
in the same Parquet file. It is the simplest possible temporal task and serves
as both a sanity check and a baseline for more complex models.

### 5.1 Sklearn Baseline

Train on a flat sample of windows (no DataLoader needed at this stage):

```python
# Build X (inputs) and Y (targets) from a sample of replays
X, Y = [], []
for replay in dataset.load_sample(200, seed=42):
    arr = replay.frames.to_numpy(dtype=np.float32)
    X.append(arr[:-1])   # all frames except last
    Y.append(arr[1:])    # all frames except first

X = np.concatenate(X)   # (total_frames, features)
Y = np.concatenate(Y)

from sklearn.linear_model import Ridge
from sklearn.preprocessing import StandardScaler

scaler = StandardScaler()
X_norm = scaler.fit_transform(X)

model = Ridge(alpha=1.0)
model.fit(X_norm, Y)
```

### 5.2 Evaluation

Per-feature mean squared error:

```python
Y_pred = model.predict(X_val_norm)
mse_per_feature = ((Y_val - Y_pred) ** 2).mean(axis=0)
```

Interpret results:
- Which features are easy to predict (low MSE)? Likely: boost (slow-changing), position
- Which are hard? Likely: velocity (discontinuous at collisions), rotation
- Is the linear model actually doing better than a trivial "predict no change" baseline?

Trivial baseline: $\hat{y}_{t+1} = y_t$ (predict no change). Compute its MSE too.

### 5.3 What Good Looks Like

At this stage, success means:
1. The pipeline runs end-to-end without errors
2. The linear model beats the trivial baseline on at least some features
3. You can inspect per-feature errors and understand why some are harder

---

## Phase 6: Scaling to Full Dataset with PyTorch

**Goal**: Train on all 7,300 replays using `WindowedReplayDataset` + `DataLoader`.

### 6.1 Setup

```python
from torch.utils.data import DataLoader
from impulse.replay_dataset import WindowedReplayDataset, ReplayDataset

dataset = ReplayDataset(db_path='./impulse.db', s3_manager=s3, cache_dir='/data/parsed')
windowed = WindowedReplayDataset(dataset, window_size=1, stride=4, cache_size=32)

loader = DataLoader(windowed, batch_size=2048, shuffle=True,
                    num_workers=4, pin_memory=True)
```

Note: `window_size=1, stride=4` gives one-frame samples (for next-frame regression,
you need to handle the target shift inside the training loop or use `window_size=2`
and split into input/target inside the model).

### 6.2 Training Loop (PyTorch Linear Model)

```python
import torch
import torch.nn as nn

class LinearPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features):
        super().__init__()
        self.linear = nn.Linear(n_features, n_features)

    def forward(self, x):
        return self.linear(x)
```

Train with MSE loss, Adam optimizer, and a learning rate scheduler.

### 6.3 Storage Considerations

For training on EC2, all 7,300 files need to be local or very fast to access:
- **EBS gp3 volume (~50 GB, ~$4/month)**: mount once, download all Parquet files,
  train repeatedly. Best option for iterative training.
- **Instance store NVMe**: fastest, but ephemeral — re-download on every restart.
- **Direct S3 streaming**: fine for EDA, slow for training (network bottleneck).

Recommendation: use an EBS volume with `cache_dir` pointing to it.

---

## Phase 7: Model Complexity Ladder

Once the linear baseline is working, progress along this ladder:

### 7.1 MLP

Replace the linear layer with a small feedforward network:

```python
class MLPPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features, hidden=256):
        super().__init__()
        self.net = nn.Sequential(
            nn.Linear(n_features, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, hidden),
            nn.ReLU(),
            nn.Linear(hidden, n_features),
        )
```

The MLP can learn nonlinear interactions but still treats each frame independently.

### 7.2 Multi-Step Input Window

Instead of predicting from a single frame, use a window of $k$ past frames:

- Change `WindowedReplayDataset(window_size=k+1)` and split in the training loop:
  `x = window[:k], y = window[k]`
- The input to the model becomes `(batch, k, features)` — a sequence
- An MLP can still handle this by flattening: `(batch, k * features)`

### 7.3 RNN / LSTM

Natural next step after multi-step input:

```python
class LSTMPredictor(nn.Module):
    def __init__(self, n_features, hidden=256, n_layers=2):
        super().__init__()
        self.lstm = nn.LSTM(n_features, hidden, n_layers, batch_first=True)
        self.head = nn.Linear(hidden, n_features)

    def forward(self, x):
        # x: (batch, seq_len, features)
        out, _ = self.lstm(x)
        return self.head(out[:, -1, :])  # predict from last hidden state
```

LSTMs can model long-range temporal dependencies (e.g., player intent, play patterns).
Expect training to be significantly slower — use GPU.

### 7.4 Transformer

After LSTM, try a causal Transformer encoder:
- Self-attention over the input window
- Can be faster than LSTM on GPU due to parallelism
- Requires more hyperparameter tuning (heads, layers, dropout)

---

## Phase 8: Supervised Tasks

Once the forecasting pipeline is solid, tackle supervised tasks that require labels.

### 8.1 Outcome Prediction

**Task**: predict which team wins (binary classification) from the game state at a given
time point or aggregate statistics.

**Labels**: extract from Ballchasing metadata (blue/orange score at end of game).
A replay is a blue win if blue score > orange score at game end.

**Features**: aggregate statistics (mean/std/max per team per feature) — no temporal
model needed for a baseline.

**Metrics**: accuracy, AUC-ROC. Baseline: always predict the team with more boost.

### 8.2 Region Classification

**Task**: classify a replay by the region it was played in (NA, EU, SAM, MENA, OCE, SSA, APAC).

**Labels**: extract from the group hierarchy path in the S3 key or Ballchasing metadata.

**Approach**:
- Start with aggregate statistics + logistic regression (no sequence model needed)
- Then try sequence-level classification with LSTM or Transformer encoder + classification head

**Caveat**: this task is easier than it sounds (arena selection differs by region, team
playstyles differ) but harder than it looks (7 unbalanced classes, some regions have
fewer games). Verify class balance before training.

### 8.3 Event Detection

**Task**: predict binary events — "a goal will occur in the next $k$ seconds" — as a
per-frame classification problem.

This is a harder task but naturally fits the sliding window framework and could be
useful for downstream analysis tools.

---

## Phase 9: Infrastructure Improvements for Scale

As the dataset and models grow:

- **Pre-converted format**: convert all Parquet files to memory-mapped numpy arrays
  (`np.memmap`) or WebDataset `.tar` shards for faster I/O at very large scale (100k+ replays)
- **Feature engineering pipeline**: add a preprocessing step that saves normalized arrays
  so normalization doesn't happen at training time
- **Distributed training**: if a single GPU is insufficient, move to multi-GPU with
  `DistributedDataParallel` or use a managed training service
- **Experiment tracking**: add MLflow or Weights & Biases for tracking runs, metrics,
  and model artifacts
- **Model versioning**: save model checkpoints and normalization stats together so
  inference uses the same preprocessing as training

---

## Complexity / Payoff Summary

| Phase | Complexity | Payoff |
|---|---|---|
| DB-level EDA | Very low | Understand dataset health and structure |
| Schema inspection | Very low | Know your features |
| Feature EDA | Low | Inform preprocessing and modeling |
| Preprocessing | Medium | Correctness matters; do this carefully |
| Linear regression baseline | Low | End-to-end pipeline working |
| PyTorch training loop | Medium | Full-scale training on 7k replays |
| MLP | Low delta | Immediate nonlinear improvement |
| Multi-step input | Low delta | Temporal context |
| LSTM | Medium | Long-range temporal modeling |
| Transformer | High | State-of-the-art sequence modeling |
| Outcome prediction | Medium | Meaningful supervised task |
| Region classification | Medium | Fun; interpretable |
| Event detection | High | Useful downstream; complex labels |
